sentences.text: The forty-seven second windows were not random.
Mapped against UTC, they formed a pattern that Marcus recognized but could not immediately name — the same way you recognize a melody three bars in before you know what song it is.
He built a correlation matrix, running the advertisement timestamps against seventeen external data sources.
The seventeenth — a dataset he had no authorization to access and accessed anyway — returned a correlation coefficient of 0.94.
The ghost ASNs weren't probing the routing table.

They were synchronized to a measurement cadence that could only make sense if someone was using BGP advertisements as a covert timing channel — broadcasting availability windows for infrastructure that officially did not exist.
The forty-seven seconds was not a duration.
Marcus sat in the dark for a while after that.
Then he encrypted the log, wrapped it in three layers of keys he had generated himself, and put it somewhere that wasn't his work machine or his home network.
He wasn't sure yet what he was going to do with what he knew.
But he had learned enough about the kind of people who build this kind of infrastructure to know that deciding slowly, in the dark, with as few witnesses as possible, was probably wise.
